options: A loosely-bound exotic nucleus (e.g. $^{11}$Li, $^{11}$Be, $^6$He) where one or two weakly-bound valence neutrons tunnel far beyond the core, forming a diffuse 'halo' and giving an anomalously large radius — a quantum-tunnelling, threshold phenomenon near the drip line
A nucleus with an unusually small radius (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
A stable nucleus on the valley of stability (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception)
A nucleus made entirely of protons (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Discovered via the large interaction cross-section of $^{11}$Li (Tanihata, 1985), halo nuclei have valence nucleons so weakly bound (separation energy ~0.3 MeV for $^{11}$Li) that their wavefunctions extend far outside the classically-allowed region — a macroscopic quantum tunnelling effect. $^{11}$Li ($^9$Li core + 2 neutrons) is a 'Borromean' two-neutron halo: no pair of its three subsystems is bound, yet the whole is — like Borromean rings. Halos appear near the neutron (and rarely proton) drip line where binding vanishes. They challenge the shell model, exhibit soft dipole modes, decoupled core+halo dynamics, and require few-body and continuum descriptions. Studied with radioactive beams, they are testbeds for nuclear forces at extreme isospin and weak binding.

active.prompt: Match each exotic-nucleus phenomenon with its description:

pairs.definition: Weakly-bound valence neutron(s) tunnelling to large radius; e.g. $^{11}$Li, $^{11}$Be
Three-body bound state with no bound two-body subsystem; e.g. $^6$He, $^{11}$Li
Excess of neutrons at the surface of neutron-rich nuclei; linked to symmetry energy
Magic numbers shift far from stability (N=20 breaks, N=16/34 emerge)

active.explanation: Far from stability, nuclei behave unexpectedly: halos and skins, vanishing/emerging magic numbers driven by the tensor and three-nucleon forces, new collective modes, and proximity to the particle-emission continuum. Rare-isotope facilities (FRIB, RIBF, ISOLDE) produce these short-lived species to test nuclear theory in uncharted territory.

question: What is the neutron skin and why is it measured?

options: In neutron-rich nuclei the neutron distribution extends slightly beyond the proton distribution, forming a thin neutron skin; its thickness correlates strongly with the density dependence of the nuclear symmetry energy, linking lab measurements (e.g. PREX on $^{208}$Pb) to neutron-star radii
A layer of protons outside the neutrons (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
An electronic shell of the atom (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general)
A skin that has no physical consequences (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: The neutron-skin thickness $\Delta R_{np} = R_n - R_p$ measures how far the surplus neutrons push out relative to protons. It is governed by the symmetry energy and especially its slope parameter $L$ (the pressure of neutron matter): a stiffer symmetry energy pushes neutrons outward, thickening the skin. Because the same symmetry energy sets the pressure that supports neutron stars, a thicker skin implies larger neutron-star radii — a direct lab-to-cosmos link. The PREX/PREX-II experiments at Jefferson Lab measured the $^{208}$Pb skin cleanly via parity-violating electron scattering (which probes the neutron distribution through the weak charge), finding a relatively thick skin and a somewhat stiff symmetry energy, in mild tension with some astrophysical inferences. CREX ($^{48}$Ca) added complementary data. Pinning down the symmetry energy is a major goal connecting nuclear structure, heavy-ion collisions, and neutron-star physics.

question: How do magic numbers change far from stability?

options: Traditional shell gaps can disappear and new ones appear in very neutron-rich nuclei: e.g. the N=20 magic number collapses (the 'island of inversion' around $^{32}$Mg, which is deformed), while N=16 and N=34 emerge as new magic numbers — driven by the tensor force and three-nucleon forces reshaping single-particle energies
Magic numbers are universal and never change (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect)
All nuclei lose magicity entirely far from stability (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Magic numbers only matter for stable nuclei (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: The shell structure that fixes magic numbers is not immutable: single-particle energies depend on which orbitals are occupied, through the spin-isospin (tensor) part of the nuclear force and three-nucleon forces. As neutron number grows for fixed proton number, these monopole shifts can erase classic gaps and open new ones. The textbook case is the 'island of inversion' near $^{31,32}$Na/Mg, where the N=20 gap collapses and intruder (pf-shell) configurations make the ground states deformed rather than spherical. Conversely, new magic numbers N=16 (e.g. $^{24}$O, the heaviest bound oxygen — the 'oxygen anomaly' explained by three-nucleon forces) and N=34 (e.g. $^{54}$Ca) emerge. Mapping shell evolution across the chart is a central mission of rare-isotope facilities and a stringent test of ab-initio nuclear forces.

question: What are rare-isotope beam facilities and what do they enable?

options: Accelerator complexes (FRIB, RIKEN RIBF, GSI/FAIR, ISOLDE) that produce short-lived exotic nuclei by fragmentation or fission of high-energy beams (or ISOL methods) and study them in flight or after re-acceleration, mapping nuclear structure, reactions, and astrophysical processes far from stability
Telescopes for observing distant galaxies (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Reactors that only make stable isotopes (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Devices that study only the proton (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Most of the ~7000 predicted bound nuclei are unstable and absent on Earth, so they must be made on demand. Rare-isotope facilities create them by smashing intense heavy-ion beams into targets (projectile fragmentation/in-flight fission, as at FRIB and RIBF) or by the ISOL (isotope separation on-line) technique (ISOLDE), then separate and either study the fast fragments or stop and re-accelerate them. They enable measurements of masses, half-lives, radii, moments, decay modes, and reaction cross-sections of nuclei near and at the drip lines — the inputs needed to test nuclear forces (ab-initio theory), discover new phenomena (halos, skins, shell evolution, new isotopes/elements), and provide the data that astrophysical models (r-process, X-ray bursts, supernovae) critically require. FRIB (Michigan State, 2022) is the current flagship, aiming to reach and define the neutron drip line for many elements.

question: What are nuclear isomers and the special case of the thorium-229 isomer?

options: Isomers are long-lived excited nuclear states (hindered decay by large spin change or shape difference); $^{229}$Th has a uniquely low-lying isomer at only ~8.4 eV — accessible to lasers, enabling a proposed ultra-precise 'nuclear clock' and tests of fundamental-constant variation
Isomers are stable ground states (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
The thorium isomer lies at MeV energies, unreachable by lasers (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Isomers decay instantly and cannot be used (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Nuclear isomers are metastable excited states whose decay is strongly hindered — usually because emitting a photon would require a large change in angular momentum (high multipole) or a big shape change, dramatically slowing the transition (half-lives from nanoseconds to, for $^{180m}$Ta, longer than the age of the universe). The $^{229}$Th isomer is extraordinary: it sits at only ~8.4 eV above the ground state, the lowest known nuclear excitation, putting it in the vacuum-ultraviolet laser range. This opens the prospect of a nuclear optical clock, potentially more stable and far less sensitive to external fields than atomic clocks (the nucleus is tiny and well-shielded), and exquisitely sensitive to possible time-variation of fundamental constants (the fine-structure constant, strong coupling) because the transition energy results from a near-cancellation of huge Coulomb and strong contributions. Recent (2023-2024) laser excitation and frequency measurements of the $^{229}$Th transition mark major steps toward this nuclear clock — a frontier blending nuclear, atomic, and metrology physics.

question: What are hypernuclei and what do they probe?

options: Nuclei in which one or more nucleons is replaced by a hyperon (a baryon containing a strange quark, e.g. $\Lambda$); free of Pauli blocking from nucleons, the hyperon probes deep nuclear states and the hyperon-nucleon force, informing the dense-matter EoS and the neutron-star 'hyperon puzzle'
Nuclei made only of electrons (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Nuclei with extra photons bound inside (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect)
Ordinary nuclei with no strange quarks (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: A hypernucleus contains at least one hyperon — a baryon with one or more strange quarks (most commonly the $\Lambda$, $uds$). Because the $\Lambda$ is distinguishable from nucleons, it is not Pauli-blocked and can occupy any single-particle orbital, acting as a clean probe of the nuclear interior and of the poorly-known hyperon-nucleon (and hyperon-hyperon) interactions. Hypernuclei are produced in strangeness-exchange or associated-production reactions ($K^-$, $\pi^+$, electron beams) and studied via gamma spectroscopy and decay. They matter for dense matter: if hyperons appear in neutron-star cores (energetically favourable once the nucleon chemical potential is high enough), they soften the equation of state and would lower the maximum mass below the observed ~2 $M_\odot$ — the 'hyperon puzzle'. Resolving it requires accurate hyperon interactions (now being computed in lattice QCD and constrained by hypernuclear data), making hypernuclear physics a key bridge between strangeness, QCD, and neutron-star astrophysics.
